answer:
In the Falsafa Urdu corpus—comprising poetic collections by Allama Iqbal (*Bang-e-Dara* Parts 1–3), Mirza Ghalib (*Diwan-e-Ghalib*), and Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq (*Diwan-e-Zauq*)—*'sabr'* (صبر, denoting patience, forbearance, or steadfast endurance) is a recurrent motif, primarily in the classical Urdu ghazal tradition and Iqbal's philosophical verse. It is not treated as a systematic philosophical concept but evoked poetically, often in contexts of romantic/ishqī longing, separation (*furqat*), divine or worldly trials (*imtihān* or *āzmā*), and spiritual resilience. 'Sabr' is frequently compounded as *sabr-āzmā* (patience-testing), *sabr-talab* (demanding patience), or paired with virtues like *istidlāl* (independence) or *shakīb* (composure), contrasting restless desire (*tamannā*, *betaab*). English translations render it as "patience," "trial of patience," or similar; transliterations preserve the Urdu term.[^1][^2][^3]

### In Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (Part 1)
Iqbal links *sabr* to the spiritual traveler's journey, exile from homeland/garden (*chaman*), and divine testing amid self-realization:
- It manifests as an ordained trial (*imtihān*): "Like the fragrance of a flower, I have left the garden's keep; / A trial of my patience has been ordained for me."[^4]
- As cultivated virtue yielding communal fruit: "the harvest of patience (*sabr*) and independence (*istiqlāl*) is this very [gathering]."[^5]

### In Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*
Ghalib, master of ghazal irony and existential depth, portrays *sabr* as love's demanding restraint amid suffering, futile longing, and life's absurd trials:
- Love requires it, even as desire rebels: "Love demands patience (*aashiqui sabr-talab*) and [yet] longing is restless (*tamanna betaab*)."[^6]
- Life's promises test it vainly: "No head [survives] from the patience-testing promise (*waada-e-sabr-āzmā*); / Where's the leisure that anyone should desire you?"[^7]
- Other instances evoke patient complaint (*sabr-e-garaz-pā*), composure in gatherings (*sabr-e-ahl-e-anjuman*), or endurance's limits.[^8]

### In Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's *Diwan-e-Zauq*
Zauq employs *sabr* in classical style, tied to heartbreak, old age, and contentment (*qanāʿat*):
- Lost amid inner turmoil: "Left no patience (*sabr*) in the heart nor rest nor resolve."[^9]
- Paired with strength (*tāqat*) in calamity: "In those disaster-stuck flights of yours, / Patience and strength's feet are uprooted there."[^10]
- In maturity or sufficiency: "We too, behold, are patience and contentment-folk (*sabr-o-qanāʿat wāle*)."[^11]

No Urdu work in the corpus offers a prose treatise on *sabr*; it emerges through ~15 poetic invocations as an active, tested virtue essential to the lover's or seeker's path, blending Sufi endurance with human frailty. Iqbal elevates it toward collective/national resilience, while Ghalib/Zauq dwell on personal anguish.

[^1]: Search hits cluster in transliterations (Romanized Urdu), e.g., *sabr-āzmā* (14/15 results); full translations use English glosses. No direct "patience/forbearance" in English bodies.
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 23 (*Farishte*), transliteration, [p-805528](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/23-farishte/transliteration/#p-805528).
[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, Ch. 3 (*Aah ko chahiye...*), transliteration, [p-32170d](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/transliteration/#p-32170d).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 23 (*Farishte*), translation, [p-3e6c44](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/23-farishte/translation/#p-3e6c44).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 38 (*Sayed Ki Loh-e-Turbat*), transliteration, [p-e91825](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/transliteration/#p-e91825).
[^6]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, Ch. 3, transliteration, [p-32170d](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/03-aah-ko-chahiye-ik-umr-asar-hote-tak/transliteration/#p-32170d).
[^7]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, Ch. 89 (*Jab tak dahan-e-zakhm...*), transliteration, [p-b84f10](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/89-jab-tak-dahan-e-zakhm-na-paida-kare-koi/transliteration/#p-b84f10).
[^8]: E.g., Ch. 104 (*Kahoon jo haal...*), transliteration; Ch. 81 (*Huzoor-e-shah...*), [p-5c7c9b](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/81-huzoor-e-shah-mein-ahl-e-sukhan-ki-aazmaish-hai/transliteration/#p-5c7c9b).
[^9]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, Ch. 5 (*Ae Zauq waqt naale...*), transliteration, [p-dbda21](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/05-ae-zauq-waqt-naale-ke-rakh-le-jigar-pe-haath/transliteration/#p-dbda21).
[^10]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, Ch. 48 (*Tere aafat-zada...*), transliteration.
[^11]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-e-Zauq*, Ch. 60 (*kya gharaz...*), transliteration, [p-7497d5](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/60-kya-gharaz-laakh-khudaai-mein-hon-daulat-waale/transliteration/#p-7497d5).
